title: Make Scrambled Eggs
summary: Make scrambled eggs that taste good and are fully cooked.
learnerPromise: Make scrambled eggs that taste good and are fully cooked.
whyItMatters: Scrambled eggs are a quick, affordable meal you can make with just a pan and a stove. Learning the basic technique gives you a reliable option for breakfast, lunch, or dinner.
realLifeUses: Making a quick breakfast before heading out.
Cooking a protein-rich meal when the fridge is almost empty.
Preparing a simple dinner when you do not feel like a complex recipe.
Adding eggs to rice, toast, or vegetables for a more filling dish.
youWillLearn: Crack eggs cleanly into a bowl without shell pieces.
Whisk eggs until the yolk and white are fully mixed.
Cook eggs in a pan with the right heat and timing.
Tell when scrambled eggs are fully cooked and ready to eat.
miniChallenge: Crack two eggs into a bowl, whisk them, and cook them in a pan.
steps: Crack two eggs into a clean bowl. Check for shell pieces and remove ones you find.
Use a fork or whisk to beat the eggs until the yolks and whites are blended.
Place a non-stick pan on the stove over low to medium heat and add a small amount of butter or oil.
Pour the eggs into the pan. Let them sit for a few seconds, then gently stir with a spatula.
Keep stirring slowly until the eggs look firm and no longer runny. Remove from heat and serve.
completionCriteria: The eggs are solid and no liquid is visible.
They are evenly cooked with no burnt or raw spots.
commonProblems: The eggs are sticking to the pan — add more butter or oil, or lower the heat.
They are cooking too fast and browning — reduce the heat and stir more often.
The texture is rubbery — remove the eggs from heat just before they look fully done; they keep cooking on the plate.
tips: Use low heat for softer, creamier eggs.
A silicone or rubber spatula works well for gentle stirring.
You can add a pinch of salt or a splash of milk for extra flavor and softness.
helpBoundary: Ask for help if you are unsure how to use the stove. Turn pot handles inward so they do not get bumped.
